coredata.dc:title: Modulation of red organic room-temperature phosphorescence in heavy atom-free phosphors
coredata.dc:creator: Liao, Fan
Du, Jiajun
Nie, Xiancheng
Wu, Ziye
Su, Hao
Huang, Wenhuan
Wang, Tao
Chen, Biao
Jiang, Jun
Zhang, Xuepeng
Zhang, Guoqing
coredata.dc:description: Red room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) was conventionally obtained by incorporating heavy metal ions or halogens. However, heavy metals are toxic and expensive while C-halogen bonds are usually prone to photobleaching. Meanwhile, heavy atoms often lead to much reduced RTP lifetimes, precluding their use in certain fields. Herein, a series of naphthalene diimides with various electron-donor substituents on the nitrogen atoms were used to achieve red RTP. It was found that a moderate intramolecular charge transfer (CT) state provides mediating intersystem crossing channels for promoting red RTP with quantum yields circa. 5%. However, much stronger donors can in fact inhibit all forms of luminescence emission including RTP via photo-induced electron transfer quenching at room temperature, in which case the red phosphorescence could be restored if the donor, such as an amine, is suppressed by protonation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Room-temperature phosphorescence
Charge transfer
Triplet state
Red/near infrared
Naphthalene diimide

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) 3D structure of NDI 1–6 (hydrogens are omitted for clarity). 1–6 were modified with benzene, 4-methybezene, 2,6-dimethybenzene, 1,3,5-trimethylbenzene, 2,6-diisopropylbenzene and 4-methoxybenzene, respectively. (b) Left: normalized absorption spectra of 1–6 in CH2Cl2. Right: steady-state emission spectra of 1–6 in PMMA film (dye/PMMA, wt/wt, 5/1000) at RT in vacuo. Insert: corresponding photos of 1–6 in PMMA film at RT in vacuo (λ ex = 365 nm). (c): RTP decay curves at emission maximum and fitted lifetime values of 1–6, respectively.

(a) Calculated energy levels of the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of the substituents [(a)–(f) for D1-D6, respectively], the NDI molecules [(a)–(f) for NDI 1–6, respectively], compared to that of the NDI core (A).
The calculated energy levels for the singlet and triplet excited states of A, NDI 2 and 4, respectively, including the oscillator strength (f) of the lowest singlet excited states.

(a) Left: chemical structure of NDI 7 and 8. Insert: Schematic illustration of photo-induced electron transfer. (b) Steady-state emission spectra of 4 and 8 in CH2Cl2 at 77 K with and without tosylic acid, respectively (λ ex = 365 nm). (c) Calculated energy levels of HOMO and LUMO of substituents for D7-D8, the NDI molecules NDI 7–8, respectively, compared to that of the NDI core (A).

(a) Schematic illustration for luminescence behavior of unsubstituted naphthalene diimide (A) and naphthalene diimide covalently linked electron donor (D) on the nitrogen atoms in polymer matrix, respectively. (b) Schematic illustration for energy levels of the orbitals (up) and the electronic transition states (bottom) of the two structures, respectively.

head.ce:title: Modulation of red organic room-temperature phosphorescence in heavy atom-free phosphors

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hefei National Laboratory for Physical Sciences at the Microscale, University of Science and Technology of China, Hefei, 230026, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hefei National Laboratory for Physical Sciences at the Microscale
University of Science and Technology of China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hefei National Laboratory for Physical Sciences at the Microscale, University of Science and Technology of China, Hefei, 230026, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Red room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) was conventionally obtained by incorporating heavy metal ions or halogens. However, heavy metals are toxic and expensive while C-halogen bonds are usually prone to photobleaching. Meanwhile, heavy atoms often lead to much reduced RTP lifetimes, precluding their use in certain fields. Herein, a series of naphthalene diimides with various electron-donor substituents on the nitrogen atoms were used to achieve red RTP. It was found that a moderate intramolecular charge transfer (CT) state provides mediating intersystem crossing channels for promoting red RTP with quantum yields circa. 5%. However, much stronger donors can in fact inhibit all forms of luminescence emission including RTP via photo-induced electron transfer quenching at room temperature, in which case the red phosphorescence could be restored if the donor, such as an amine, is suppressed by protonation.

ce:section.ce:para: Room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials have great application potential in various fields such as anti-counterfeiting, data encryption, chemical sensing, high-efficiency optoelectronic devices and time-resolved bioimaging [1–5]. Among them, purely organic RTP materials have attracted intense research interests due to multiple advantages over their organometallic counterparts, such as lower cost, mitigated toxicity, better processability and increased stability against moisture [6–10]. A necessary requirement to realize organic molecular RTP is to suppress non-radiative decay processes which compete with the RTP decay. To this end, a rigid and oxygen-free local environment for phosphors is usually essential, where crystalline and amorphous media have both been developed [11–27]. A more fundamental prerequisite is to efficiently populate triplet excited states via intersystem crossing (ISC) following electronic excitation to boost RTP. In this regard, the most conventional strategies are based on heavy atom effect and El-Sayed's rule [3,5].

Despite tremendous efforts in this field, the vast majority of the reported organic RTP materials exhibit emission maxima in the green-yellow range [5]. For applications such bioimaging, it is well-known that red/near infrared (NIR) RTP will be much more attractive since it has better penetration ability in tissues than emissions of shorter wavelengths. However, red/NIR RTP in purely organic system is rarely obtained so far, presumably due to the energy gap law that exponentially reduces the quantum yield as the emission is red-shifted. This is particularly the case for RTP emission given its even lower decay rate compared to that of fluorescence, which makes it almost impossible to compete with thermal decay in the red region. In this sense, the naphthalene diimide (NDI) core structure is an ideal red phosphor given its large conjugation (low energy of triplet excited state), rigid structure (small intrinsic non-radiative decay tendency), and high symmetry (low vibronic deactivation). However, red RTP based on NDI derivatives has been typically achieved by incorporating carbon-halogen (C-X) bond, which is prone to photobleaching due to a lowered σ* bond. Although the NDI core contains carbonyl group, the rigid conformation prohibits efficient n-π* transition realized by in-plane and out-of-plane mixing of orbitals via wagging motions from the carbonyl group. Therefore, an alternative strategy to facilitate ISC is required to obtain more stable red RTP NDIs.

Herein, as shown in Scheme 1 , the red RTP of unsubstituted NDI core is inefficient with a main π-π* character in the UV region with large energy gap between the excited singlet and triplet states (ΔE ST), while the energy level of the 3π-π* state is located in the red range (>600 nm). By modulating the NDI core with organic electron donating groups, intramolecular charge transfer (CT) state could be introduced. The CT state could act as a mediating state for the ISC process since the ΔE ST for CT state is substantially smaller than that of the initial π-π* state of the NDI aromatic core [4]. In this way, the molecules should undergo a decay pathway 1π-π* → 1CT → 3CT → 3π-π* → S0 after photoexcitation and thus efficient red RTP can be realized. Phenyl-based donors with different substituent groups were used to test how electronic and steric effects influence the RTP properties and our design strategy was then substantiated both experimentally and theoretically.

NDIs 1–6 (Fig. 1 a) with different donors (benzene, 4-methybezene, 2,6-dimethybenzene, 1,3,5-trimethylbenzene, 2,6-diisopropylbenzene and 4-methoxybenzene for 1–6, respectively) on both nitrogen atoms were synthesized via condensation reactions between 1,4,5,8-naphthalenetetracarboxylic dianhydride (NDA) and corresponding amines. All products were characterized by 1H NMR and high-resolution mass spectroscopy (Supporting information, Figure S1-12). The optical properties of the NDIs were then systematically investigated. To minimize scattering and self-absorption effect, UV–vis absorption spectra of the NDIs were acquired in dilute CH2Cl2 solutions, given other polar solvents tend to have stronger solvation effect on the D-A-D structure. The RTP spectra were obtained in deoxygenated solid-state films given that triplet excited states could easily be quenched in fluid conditions and by a trace amount of molecular oxygen. As shown in Fig. 1b (left), UV–vis absorption spectra of 1–6 in CH2Cl2 all show highly resolved vibronic peaks around 340, 360 and 380 nm (ε > 10,000 M−1cm−1). These intense absorption peaks are ascribed to π-π* transitions of the central NDI aromatic ring with clear vibrational energy gap of ~1500 cm−1. In comparison, the weak absorption between 250 nm–300 nm of NDI 1–5 were ascribed to benzene ring and methoxy group on the benzene ring accounts for a much more intense peak between 250 nm–300 nm for NDI 6.

Luminescence properties of 1–6 in polymer matrices which provide a rigid environment necessary for RTP were then investigated. The NDIs 1–6 and PMMA (0.5%, dye/PMMA, wt/wt) were co-dissolved in CH2Cl2 and the solvent was totally removed in reduced pressure to prepare clear films (ca. 0.2 mm) cast on quartz substrates. In the air, the films were non-luminescent under 365-nm light illumination and the steady-state emission spectra recorded on the spectrofluorometer were almost baselines. When the films were degassed and sealed in quartz tubes, however, notable red emissions for 1–6 could be observed when illuminated by 365-nm UV light, indicating oxygen-sensitive RTP presence. As shown in Fig. 1b (right), the steady-state emission spectra of 1–6 in vacuo all show three highly resolved vibrational peaks around 610 nm, 670 nm and 745 nm. These emission peaks are good mirror images of the absorption spectra and vibrational progressions of the NDI ring are clear, i.e., the three peaks are separated by ~1500 cm−1, indicating the NDI-localized 3π-π* transitions. As shown in Fig. 1c, all time-resolved photoluminescence decay curves at the emission maxima of 1–6 were fitted to a triple-exponential kinetics, indicating the heterogeneous microenvironment of the phosphors in PMMA matrix. The weighted average lifetime values of 1–6 were all calculated to be several tens of milliseconds (34.69 ms, 35.5 ms, 34.95 ms, 35.83 ms, 34.45 ms and 30.87 ms for 1–6, respectively) and thus are determined to be phosphorescence, which are orders of magnitude longer than that of traditional red phosphors based on Ir, Pt, Ru, Pd, or Eu organometallic complexes. Simultaneously, the quantum yields of the red RTP of these heavy atom-free NDIs are quite notable, i.e., 5.0%, 4.3%, 4.3%, 5.1%, 4.9% and 4.4% for 1–6, respectively. Given the quenching propensity of heavy-atom-free triplet excitons close to the ground state, the red RTP of the NDIs investigated here are already quite efficient.

To shed light on the luminescence properties of the NDI system, the first-principle calculations (Fig. 2 ) on energy levels of the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) were carried out for the NDI core (H-substitution at the nitrogen site, A), the electron-donating substituents D1-D6 as well as NDI 1–6. The calculations were performed on a Gaussian 09 software at the B3LYP/6-31G (d) level. The HOMO and LUMO energy levels of A are −6.99 eV and −3.38 eV, respectively, with the gap to be 3.61 eV. The HOMO of independent donor groups are −6.81 eV, −6.52 eV, −6.37 eV, −6.32 eV, −6.41, −6.00 eV for D1 (benzene), D2 (4-methybezene), D3 (2,6-dimethybenzene), D4 (1,3,5-trimethylbenzene), D5 (2,6-diisopropylbenzene) and D6 (4-methoxybenzene), respectively. Meanwhile, the LUMO of the donors are −0.01 eV, 0.02 eV, 0.04 eV, 0.15 eV, 0.01 eV, −0.05 eV for D1-D6, respectively, resulting in gap between HOMO and LUMO to be 6.80 eV, 6.54 eV, 6.41 eV, 6.46 eV, 6.42 eV, 5.95 eV, respectively. The LUMOs of NDI 1–6 are −3.33 eV, −3.31 eV, −3.35 eV, −3.33 eV, −3.36 eV and −3.31 eV, respectively, which are very close to the initial value of the unsubstituted NDI core (−3.38 eV). This can be ascribed to nearly non-interacting, orthogonal orbitals between the N-donor and the NDI core due to steric effects. However, the HOMOs of the NDIs exhibit a much more significant dependence on the N-substituents. In general, the higher the HOMO of the independent donors, the higher the HOMO of NDI. Specifically, the HOMOs of the NDIs are −6.92 eV, −6.77 eV, −6.66 eV, −6.57 eV, −6.57 eV and −6.23 eV for NDI 1–6, respectively. Indeed, the HOMOs of 2–6 almost exclusively consist of contribution from HOMOs of the N-substituents. Meanwhile, the energy gaps between the HOMO and LUMO of NDI 1–6 close in compared to A, being 3.59 eV, 3.46 eV, 3.31 eV, 3.24 eV, 3.21 eV, and 2.93 eV for 1–6, respectively, opening doors for CT pathways in between the 1π-π* and 3π-π* states of the NDI core.

To further substantiate our claim, density functional theory (DFT) calculations on the electronic transition states for NDI core (A) and selected NDIs 2 and 4 were carried out. As can be seen in Fig. 3 , the calculated lowest singlet (f = 0.37) and triplet excited states are of π-π* character and the ΔE ST is the largest for NDI alone without a donor presence, which makes ISC the least favorable among the three molecules. In contrast, the S1 states of 4 turn into CT state while the π-π* transition turns into higher singlet excited state. Furthermore, the oscillator strength of the π-π* transition increases to 0.52 while that of the CT state is 0.00. The results may account for the fact that the molar extinction coefficients of the π-π* transition for 1–6 are all >10,000 M−1cm−1 while the CT state is barely visible in the UV–vis absorption spectra. Despite the small oscillator strength of the CT transition, the calculated energy level of the lowest triplet excited state (3CT) is in proximity to that of 1CT with ΔE ST = 0.02 eV. Therefore, the molecule in the excited state undergoes an energy decay pathway like 1π-π* → 1CT → 3CT → 3π-π* → S0. This is also the case for NDI 2 (Fig. 3, medium) and thus proves our proposed strategy for achieving efficient red RTP in NDI with charge transfer mediation. The quantum yield values for NDIs 1–6 are close to each other and the differences are within 1%. Meanwhile, the lifetime values for 1–6 are also at the same order of magnitude and the differences are generally within 5 ms. At this point, it should be reluctant to draw a quantitative conclusion on RTP performance of these NDIs, but what is clear is that an appropriate intramolecular charge transfer state could boost NDI RTP compared to the unsubstituted dianhydride (NDA).

To further investigate how donors influence the RTP, two more NDIs with much more potent electronic donors, i.e., NDI 7 and 8 were synthesized and investigated. It was found that neither 7 nor 8 exhibit any noticeable RTP in PMMA film under degassed condition. The energy gaps between HOMO and LUMO of 7 and 8 are calculated to be 2.79 eV and 2.02 eV, respectively (Fig. 4 c), which are much lower than that of 1–6. Indeed, when the phenyl group is modified with robust donor such as 1,3,5-trimethoxybenzene or N, N-dimethyl group, the conjugated lone-pair electrons may significantly raise the HOMO level of the donor moiety which either becomes close to the HOMO energy of the NDI core or above it, resulting in photo-induced electron transfer (PET) quenching (Fig. 4a). To substantiate our claim, we studied the protonation-induced spectroscopic changes for 8, which can easily accept proton and decrease the HOMO level of the N-substitution. Shown in Fig. 4b are the steady-state emission spectra of 4 and 8 at 77 K (to eliminate thermal quenching from internal rotations of the N-substitution) in CH2Cl2. For 8, no emission could be recorded even at 77 K (baseline on the spectrometer), indicating that the PET is still effective at a much lower temperature. Upon addition of tosylic acid (TsOH) in CH2Cl2 which is capable of protonating the N-donor, a weak blue emission could be observed; when the solution was cooled to 77 K, a strong blue emission was noted under a 365-nm UV light excitation. Meanwhile, a red afterglow persisted after the UV excitation had ceased. The emission spectrum shows major fluorescence peaks around 500 nm (τ = 13 ns, Figure S17), while shoulder peaks characteristic of NDI phosphorescence are also clear at ~610 nm (τ = 67.2 ms, Figure S18) and ~670 nm. To make sure that TsOH protonation did not introduce unwanted luminescence state or impurities, a control experimental using 4 was also performed. The alkylphenyl substitution NDI cannot accept TsOH protons and therefore should not change before and after TsOH addition. Indeed, the spectrum of 4 is almost identical to that of 4/TsOH mixture in CH2Cl2 and coincides with the shoulder peak of 8 after protonation. In both cases, visually strong red afterglow was observed, indicating the prevalence of phosphorescence at both conditions. As for NDI 7, similar protonation experiment was also carried out. However, the photoluminescence spectra of 7 in CH2Cl2 before and after adding excess TsOH neither exhibited any emission peak and were both almost baselines (Figurer S19), which may be due to the fact that the nucleophilic ability of methoxy group to protons is much weaker than that of N, N-dimethyl group. Therefore, PET might still prevail in presence of acid under 77 K and lead to luminescence quenching for 7. These two “exceptions”, i.e., NDI 7 and 8, in fact agree well with our design strategy from a different perspective, that a proper donor and a CT state with matching energy level is key to the RTP performance of NDIs.

In summary, by using facilely synthesized NDIs, we systematically investigated the influence of substituents on the red/NIR RTP properties of the NDI dyes. It was found that 1) appropriately high HOMO and electron-donating abilities of the substituents can facilitate CT state from the donor to the acceptor. The CT state accelerates ISC of the NDI dye and results in stronger RTP; 2) if the HOMO of the substituent is too high, PET can occur and thus the RTP of the dye will be entirely quenched. Timed-resolved and targeting imaging experiments of these type of purely organic and heavy atom-free red RTP dyes are underway and will be published in the future.

F. Liao and J. Du synthesized and characterized the compounds. X. Nie assisted in the measurements and provided helpful discussions. Thus, F. Liao, J. Du and X. Nie contributed equally to this work. All the computational work was conducted by Z. Wu and J. Jiang. H. Su, W. Huang, T. Wang and B. Chen provided helpful discussions. X. Zhang and G. Zhang supervised this work, wrote and revised the manuscript and are responsible for the funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank the National Key R&D Program of China (2017YFA0303500 to G. Z.), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21975238 to G. Z.) and the University of Science and Technology of China startup funds (KY2340000139 to X. Z.).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A dual-emissive-materials design concept enables tumour hypoxia imaging

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Conversion of carbon dots from fluorescence to ultralong room-temperature phosphorescence by heating for security applications
